title: Risk Management with Variable Capital Utilization and Time-Varying Collateral Capacity
abstract: We build a risk management model that incorporates variable capital utilization and time-varying collateral capacity. The former lets firms optimally choose capital utilization, and hence production, which affects capital depreciation and risk exposure. The latter means firms’ ability to borrow and hedge increases with expected earnings and thus utilization. Calibrated solutions show both ingredients matter for firm value. We test the novel model predictions using a new data set of oil and gas producers. Consistent with model predictions, we find utilization is negatively correlated with firm liquidity, while hedging is positively correlated with liquidity and expected profitability.

author.Zhongjin Lu: Terry College of Business, University of Georgia, Athens, Georgia 30606
author.Siddharth Vij: Terry College of Business, University of Georgia, Athens, Georgia 30606

article.1. Introduction: We investigate theoretically and empirically the role of capital utilization in corporate risk management decisions when facing time-varying collateral capacity. Existing theories highlight the important role of investment and financing decisions in affecting firms’ hedging policies (Froot et al. 1993;Rampini and Viswanathan 2010,2013;Bolton et al. 2011). Less attention has been paid to the possibility that firms can adjust their capital utilization, and hence production, as part of their risk management strategy. This channel is potentially important: Industrial capacity utilization is an important macroeconomic indicator, and its time variation serves as a key driving force of business cycle dynamics in macroeconomics models (Greenwood et al. 1988,Jaimovich and Rebelo 2009). Our paper contributes to the literature by considering variable capital utilization (VCU) when analyzing firms’ optimal risk management policy. We develop a parsimonious and tractable model that incorporates VCU into a unified risk management framework fromBolton et al. (2011). Specifically, the VCU mechanism means that a higher capital utilization rate leads to higher current production but faster capital depreciation. Our model shows that, given liquidity (i.e., net cash position) and expected profitability, firms’ capital utilization choice affects risk management decisions through an intertemporal tradeoff channel and a risk exposure channel. There is an intertemporal tradeoff because while increasing capital utilization boosts production and thus current cash flows, the resulting higher depreciation leads to lower remaining capital, which negatively affects future cash flows holding other policies unchanged. The risk exposure channel refers to the fact that increasing production increases firms’ cash flow risk due to a higher exposure to commodity price risk. Firms facing liquidity shortages tend to prioritize immediate cash flows over future ones, which in turn leads to a rise in capital utilization and production. At the same time, such a firm is more reluctant to bear the additional risk brought by higher production, because it has fewer resources for hedging when facing a collateral constraint. Therefore, the intertemporal tradeoff and the risk exposure channel generated by VCU predict opposite relations between firm liquidity and capital utilization. In contrast, the existing risk management models assume constant capital utilization and thus cannot be used to understand the relation between liquidity management and capital utilization. Our calibration shows that without the ability to adjust utilization, firm value drops by 1.8% on average and this loss in value is larger when expected profitability is high and firm liquidity is low. Furthermore, we find that incorporating VCU into the existing risk management model changes the model prediction on firms’ optimal hedging policies. Motivated by the literature on earnings-based collateral constraints (Nikolov et al. 2019,Lian and Ma 2020,Drechsel 2023), we further add time-varying collateral capacity (TCC) to the risk management model ofBolton et al. (2011). Specifically, TCC means that time-varying expected profitability determines the capacity to borrow and hedge. This is important for understanding how firm liquidity and expected profitability are related to firms’ hedging policies. Similar to existing models (Rampini and Viswanathan 2010,2013) that feature the collateral constraint, our model predicts an overall positive correlation between hedging and the firm’s liquidity, after controlling for expected profitability. In contrast to these models, the relation between hedging and profitability depends on the relative importance of the following two effects. First, in our model, similar to the existing models with collateral constraint and persistent profitability, higher expected profitability means a better investment opportunity, which in turn necessitates more financing for this investment. As financing and hedging compete for limited collateral capacity, this investment opportunity channel predicts a negative relation between hedging and expected profitability. Second, under TCC, collateral capacity is positively related to expected profitability. This could result in a positive correlation between hedging and profitability if the positive effect of increased collateral capacity on hedging outweighs the negative effect brought about by the investment opportunity channel, when profitability is high.1Thus, the empirical relation between hedging and profitability depends on the relative importance of these two effects in the data. We then take these model predictions to the data. We construct a comprehensive panel data set on hedging positions, production, and proved reserves for independent oil and gas exploration and production (E&P) firms between 2002 and 2016. We choose these firms because both utilization and hedging can be cleanly measured at the firm-year level. We define firms’hedge ratioas the percentage of annual production volume that is hedged against downside price risk andcapital utilizationas the ratio of annual production to total proved reserves. We find that risk management is prevalent and significant for our sample firms. More than 90% of the firm-year observations in our sample have nonzero hedging. The hedge ratio has large variation across firms and over time, with the median firm-year hedge ratio being 45% and the standard deviation being 32%. Capital utilization also has substantial variation, with a standard deviation of 7% relative to a mean of 10%. Guided by our theory, we use the production-weighted spot prices of oil and natural gas as our empirical proxy for expected profitability. These prices are likely exogenous to an individual firm since they are determined in global commodity markets. Our empirical proxy for firm liquidity is cash, short-term investments, and unrealized hedging gains, net of borrowing. To address the potential endogeneity concern that our liquidity measure can be affected by unobserved firm choices outside of our model, we instrument liquidity with unrealized hedging gains and implement a two-stage least squares (2SLS) estimation. Unrealized hedging gains represent the mark-to-market profits on long-term derivative contracts and are a critical component of liquidity in practice for E&P producers. The validity of our identification strategy is based on the observation thatunpredictable changesin oil and gas prices cause differential unrealized derivative gains for different pre-existing hedging positions. We first empirically test the relation between liquidity and the capital utilization rate. We find that liquidity and the capital utilization rate are negatively correlated after controlling for expected profitability, suggesting that the intertemporal tradeoff channel is more important than the risk exposure channel in our sample. Such a relation is not predicted by existing risk management models, and, to the best of our knowledge, we are the first to document this negative correlation empirically. We then test the effect of liquidity and expected profitability on hedge ratio. We find that the hedge ratio is positively related to liquidity, supporting the important role of collateral constraint highlighted inRampini et al. (2014). We also find a significant positive relation between expected profitability and hedge ratio. This result suggests that the TCC effect is more important than the investment opportunity effect in driving firms’ hedging policy in our sample. In addition to these primary relations, our model also generates a rich set of predictions about the heterogeneous effect of liquidity and expected profitability on utilization and hedging. First, since the VCU mechanism is stronger for firms with lower liquidity, the liquidity-utilization relationship is more negative among these firms. Second, the risk exposure channel of the VCU mechanism is relatively more important when volatility is higher. This implies that the negative liquidity utilization is weaker and the positive liquidity hedging is stronger during periods of high price volatility. Third, since the collateral capacity is more variable for firms with high proved undeveloped reserves relative to total proved reserves, we expect the collateral constraints to be more binding in bad states for these firms. Thus, the negative liquidity utilization and positive hedging profitability relationships will be stronger among these firms with a high fraction of proved undeveloped reserves. We find empirical support for all these predictions. 1.1. Related Literature To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to incorporate endogenous capital utilization into the unified dynamic risk management framework ofBolton et al. (2011). We show that VCU is an important component of risk management, and it affects the optimal hedging policy. Our specification of the TCC is motivated by the literature on earnings-based borrowing constraints (Nikolov et al. 2019,Lian and Ma 2020,Drechsel 2023). Given our focus on risk management decisions, the collateral capacity in our model restricts both borrowing and hedging, similar toRampini and Viswanathan (2010,2013).2Our model contributes to the literature by showing how the interaction of VCU and TCC endogenously affect risk management decisions. Our analysis also contributes to the macroeconomics and finance literature on VCU. In macroeconomics,Keynes (1936)emphasizes the intertemporal tradeoff in capital utilization, labeling it “user cost of capital.”Greenwood et al. (1988)andJaimovich and Rebelo (2009), among others, build on this idea and make it an important ingredient in neoclassical macroeconomic theories to explain business cycles. Recent papers byGarlappi and Song (2017),Ai et al. (2020),Dou et al. (2021), andGrigoris and Segal (2024)explore the asset pricing implications of capital utilization. The fact that higher production accelerates capital depreciation is also prominently featured in the analysis ofHotelling (1931)of optimal production of exhaustible resources. We differ from all these studies by investigating the implications of VCU for firm risk management, both theoretically and empirically. Finally, our study adds to the empirical literature starting fromTufano (1996)andHaushalter (2000)that investigates the determinants of the intensive margin of hedging. We contribute to the existing empirical literature by testing the new implications of a dynamic risk management model with VCU and TCC by using exogenous variations in liquidity and expected profitability.3Several earlier studies rely on a short time series of data and are thus cross-sectional in nature. In contrast, the panel data we construct in this study enables us to control for unobserved time-invariant firm-specific heterogeneity and investigate the average pattern over both bull and bear periods of the oil and gas markets. In this respect, our paper joins the more recent empirical studies that characterize firms’ risk management practice using a long time series, such asKumar and Rabinovitch (2013),Bonaimé et al. (2014),Rampini et al. (2014),Ferriani and Veronese (2019), andBabenko et al. (2023).

First, VCU introduces an intertemporal tradeoff: A higher utilization on the one hand leads to higher current production and current cash flows but on the other hand accelerates depreciation of capital. The latter negatively affects future production and future cash flows, holding other policies unchanged. Second, the utilization rate determines the production and affects the firm’s exposure to the price riskdZp,t, which involves a tradeoff between expected cash flows and risk exposure. We refer to these two tradeoffs as the intertemporal tradeoff and the risk exposure channel, respectively. The macroeconomic literature has highlighted the importance of the VCU mechanism for understanding the business cycle. We show in later sections that incorporating the VCU mechanism into the existing risk management models is important to understand the dynamics of hedging and production under financial frictions. 2.1.3. In the first term,μsKis the cash flows generated by one unit increase of utilization, whose contribution to the firm value is affected by the marginal value of liquidityVW. Therefore, increasingucan increase the current production and liquidity, but it decreases the capital through faster depreciation and thus has a negative effect on future production and liquidity. The third term captures the risk exposure channel of VCU. The first part,σa2u∗K2−σa2H∗K, indicates that a higher utilization can increase the firm’s risk exposure to the price riskdZa,t, holding the hedging policyH∗unchanged. The second part,ρσaσfu∗Kt2, comes from the correlation between the price risk of production and the risks that are not directly affected by production. The contribution of these two parts to the value function depends on the value function curvatureVWW, which is negative as inBolton et al. (2011). This is because even though the firm is risk neutral, the possibility of liquidation at a discounted price causes the firm to be averse to the variation of liquidity. Therefore, increasing utilization negatively affects the value function via the risk exposure channel. When the collateral constraint is not binding, the price risk associated with production is completely hedged away. As a result, the third term in the optimal condition of utilization (i.e.,σa2u∗K2−σa2H∗K+ρσaσfu∗K2inEquation (12)) is zero. In this case, the risk exposure channel of utilization is shut down, and only the intertemporal tradeoff is at play. In contrast, when the collateral constraint is binding, hedging is constrained by the hedging capacity and hence both the risk exposure channel and the intertemporal tradeoff arising from VCU are at play. Therefore, our model highlights that firm’s production and hedging decisions are tightly connected when facing the collateral constraint. 2.3. Relating Model to Practice In this section, we discuss how our modeling choices are related to the general practices in the E&P industry. First, the capital in the model corresponds to the total proved reserves for E&P firms. This is because proved reserves are the primary capital of an E&P company, the definition, and the value of which are subject to SEC regulations due to their importance for investors and lenders. Specifically, the definition of proved reserves is regulated by SEC’s Rule 4-10(a) (22) of Regulation S as the total amount of oil and gas commercially recoverable from known reservoirs and under current economic conditions, operating methods, and government regulations with little doubt within five years. The proved reserves can be further categorized into producing developed proved reserves, nonproducing developed proved reserves, and undeveloped reserves, the NPV of which would be adjusted by an increasing risk discount to determine the borrowing base. In contrast, the unproved reserves, including probable and possible reserves, typically receive zero weight in the borrowing base determination. Second, we choose to use the earnings-based collateral constraint rather than the asset-based collateral constraint. These two types of collateral constraints are closely related for the E&P industry, because the borrowing base is related to the net present value of the estimated future cash flows generated by proved reserves as we discussed previously. AsLian and Ma (2020)emphasize, the defining difference between the earnings-based and asset-based collateral constraints is whether the constraint is tied to the cash flow value of the continuing operation of the restructured firm (earnings-based) or the liquidation value of physical assets (asset-based). From this perspective, we use the earnings-based collateral constraint because maintaining the value of the proved reserves requires experienced operators to continue extraction and production (OCC 2018). Third, the VCU mechanism works through the extensive margin instead of the intensive margin in the E&P industry. That is, E&P firms increase production by drilling and completing more wells. Well drilling and well completion are two independent stages in oil and gas extraction. After well completion, production at the well level follows a natural declining curve dictated by the reservoir pressure, as emphasized byAnderson et al. (2018)andGilje et al. (2020). The drilled but uncompleted wells, commonly referred to as DUCs, serve as the main medium through which the E&P firms can adjust their production by completing the DUCs at a faster or slower rate. In extreme bearish markets, producers may also shut in producing wells to slow down production.7We also want to emphasize that well completion incurs large cash outlay and the appropriate time horizon to interpret our model prediction is a year.8 Fourth, we capture the nonlinear relation between the marginal cost and utilization via a quadratic depreciation function as in ourEquation (2). Increasing the utilization by drilling more wells and accelerating the completion of DUCs increase the marginal cost of production because drawing down the reservoir pressure faster than the natural rate can impair the ultimate recoverable reserve (Xu et al. 2022). Furthermore, because E&P companies typically hire outside contractors to drill and complete wells, increasing the utilization in a short period can also be costly. Similarly, decreasing the utilization by shutting in producing wells increases the marginal cost as restoring those wells back to precurtailed volumes is costly and uncertain. In our quadratic depreciation function, firms achieve the minimum cost utilization rate atu0.

We then discuss the testable implications. 3.1. Calibration We base our calibration of parameters on the related empirical moments in our sample. Our calibration is reported inTable 1. Figure 1(g)plots the optimal capital utilization rateu∗(w,s). As discussed inSection 2.2, the intertemporal tradeoff channel and the risk exposure channel have opposite effects on the relation between liquidity and utilization. The intertemporal tradeoff predicts that when liquiditywtis low and the marginal value of liquidity is high, firms increase production by increasing the utilization rateuτoverτ∈(t,t+dt). Nonetheless, when the hedging is constrained by the collateral capacity (w<w^s), the additional risk exposure channel has the effect opposite to the intertemporal tradeoff: Low liquidity leads to less hedging and higher risk aversion, both of which could induce firms to reduce risk by reducing production. Under our current calibration, the intertemporal tradeoff dominates the risk exposure channel, leading to a general negative relation between utilization rate and liquidity. Figure 1(h)presents the optimal hedge ratioh∗(w,s). As discussed inSection 2.2, both desired hedging and hedging capacity are driving the relation between hedging and liquidityw. When the collateral constraint is not binding (w≥w^s), the firm achieves desired hedging, which is decreasing with liquidity asu∗is negatively correlated with liquidity. When the collateral constraint is binding (w<w^s), the hedging is determined by the hedging capacity, which is increasing with liquidity. Under our calibration, our model generates a nonlinear and overall positive relation between firm’s hedge ratio and liquidity given expected profitability, similar toRampini and Viswanathan (2010,2013).9On the contrary, models without collateral constraints (Froot et al. 1993) only have the desired hedging channel and thus predict a negative relation between hedging and liquidity. Our model also generates a positive relation between hedging and expected profitability. The investment opportunity channel, investment increases in expected profitability, demanding more debt financing, and crowding out the hedging, leads to a negative correlation between hedge ratio and expected profitability, similar to models with time-invariant collateral capacity (Rampini and Viswanathan 2010,2013). The collateral capacity channel, collateral capacity increases in expected profitability, allowing for more debt financing and hedging, leads to a positive correlation between hedging and expected profitability. Finally, high expected profitability leads to higher utilization, which in turn generates higher exposure to the price risk. This motivates the firm to hedge more in the good state. Empirically, we define the hedge ratio as hedging scaled by production to control for the third effect. In our calibrated benchmark model, the collateral capacity channel dominates the investment opportunity channel, and thus the optimal hedging is increasing in expected profitability. 3.3. Value of VCU for Risk Management Our benchmark model highlights that the flexible production decision is an integral part of firms’ risk management strategies. To further investigate the role of VCU in risk management, we shut down the VCU mechanism (i.e., firms cannot adjust their utilization) while maintaining the state-dependent collateral capacity. In this model without VCU (hereafter Model NV), the utilization is set at the mean utilization rate in the benchmark model. Figure 2presents the solutions and compares Model NV to the benchmark model.Figure 2, (a) and (b), presents the value functions in two states. We find that without VCU, the firm has lower value conditioning on the same values of state variables (w,s).10Furthermore, the difference in firm value is larger when the liquidity becomes lower. Table X.1 in the Online Appendix reports these values. Atw= 0.1 (near the payout boundary), the firm value in the Model NV is 2.0% (1.8%) lower than that in the benchmark model in the good (bad) state. The difference increases to 5.5% (7.2%) in the good (bad) state whenw=−0.4(around the liquidation boundary in the bad state). This difference further increases to 32.1% in the good state whenw=−0.6(around the liquidation boundary in the good state). The lower firm value in the alternative model demonstrates that the firm’s ability to adjust the utilization rate is important for risk management. Specifically, if the firm cannot increase its production to generate more operating cash flows in the low liquidity state, the liquidation risk increases. This can be seen from the fact that the firm liquidates sooner without VCU (w¯G=−0.63in the Model NV versusw¯G=−0.85in the benchmark model). Furthermore, with VCU and the earnings-based collateral constraint, the collateral capacity in the bad state is also larger as the firm increases its production. Figure 2, (c) and (d), presents the utilization policies. In Model NV, the utilization rate is constant by assumption. This contradicts the time-varying capital utilization shown by both our data and the survey of capacity utilization published on FRED.Figure 2, (e) and (f), plots the optimal hedging policies in Model NV. Compared with the benchmark model, the firm now hedges less in the good state when collateral constraint is binding. In the bad state, the collateral constraint is binding later because the fixed utilization rate in Model NV (0.097) is higher than the optimal utilization in the benchmark model (0.086). Finally, the positive hedging-liquidity relation is stronger in Model NV, because this firm relies more on hedging to manage risks when it cannot adjust production to manage liquidity. Figure 2, (g) and (h), presents the investment under Model NV. Compared with the benchmark model, the investment level is lower in Model NV and this investment difference is larger when profitability is high (s=G) and when the liquiditywis low. This is because a firm in Model NV cannot adjust its utilization across profitability states. When the liquidity is low or when profitability is high, the optimal utilization in the benchmark model is higher than that in Model NV, which leads to higher cash flows, more depreciation, and higher borrowing capacity. All three lead to higher investment in the benchmark model than in Model NV. In the bad profitability state with high liquidity, the utilization in Model NV is higher than the optimal solution in the benchmark case. Such suboptimal overproduction in Model NV leads to more negative cash flows, reducing investment in Model NV. This cash flow effect dominates the borrowing capacity effect and the depreciation effect, both of which are not important when liquidity is high. To further clarify the roles of the two novel mechanisms, VCU and TCC, we discuss two additional alternative models in Online Appendix A.2 and show one can go from a classic hedging model likeFroot et al. (1993)to our model by adding VCU and TCC step by step. Overall, our results show the inability to change the utilization rate weakens the firm’s ability to manage liquidity and risk, leading to a meaningful reduction in firm value. This implication is consistent with the fact that firms in our sample do not keep their utilization rate constant. We now turn to the predictions from our benchmark model that are empirically testable. 3.4. Testable Predictions A new prediction from the VCU mechanism is about the correlation between liquidity and the capital utilization rate, given expected profitability (Figure 1(e)). The utilization rate is negatively correlated with liquidity. Although our model generates an overall positive relation between hedge ratio and liquidity, similar to the existing models that emphasize collateral constraints (Rampini and Viswanathan 2010,2013), the time-varying nature of the collateral capacity in our model leads to a positive relation between the hedge ratio and the expected profitability (Figure 1(f)). The hedge ratio is negative correlated with liquidity. The hedge ratio is positively correlated with expected profitability. Furthermore, we exploit three empirical variations that affect the relative importance of the new mechanisms of our model. First, as our benchmark model shows firms with low liquidity rely more on the intertemporal tradeoff channel of the VCU mechanism, leading to a more negative relation between utilization and liquidity (Figure 1(e)). This gives us the following testable prediction. The negative correlation between utilization and liquidity is stronger among firms with lower liquidity. Second, the risk exposure channel of the VCU mechanism is stronger when the volatility is high. Empirically, the aggregate and idiosyncratic risks tend to increase together and correlate more strongly in high volatility periods. Therefore, we capture the volatility effect by contrasting the high (low) volatility case, in whichσa,σf, andρare 20% higher (lower) than the benchmark case. Figure 3, (a)–(d), shows that the utilization is less responsive to liquidity in the high volatility case, consistent with the increasing importance of the risk exposure channel relative to the intertemporal channel. Firms’ hedge ratio is more positively related to liquidity in the high volatility case because, when the risk exposure channel becomes more important, firms rely less on the VCU and more on hedging to manage liquidity risk. This gives us the following testable predictions. The negative relation between utilization and liquidity is weaker when volatility is higher. The positive relation between hedging and liquidity is stronger when volatility is higher. Third, the TCC channel becomes more important when the collateral capacity covaries more positively with the commodity price. Empirically, we exploit the fact that the value of proved undeveloped reserves is more sensitive to the commodity price than proved developed reserves, as the former requires higher costs to extract and is the first to be written down when the commodity price is low. Therefore, the TCC channel should be relatively more important for firms with a higher ratio of proved undeveloped reserves to total proved reserves (hereafter the PUD ratio). We model the high PUD ratio firms as those with slightly lower collateral capacity in the good state (θG=3.5) and substantially lowerθin the bad state (θB=2.75), relative to the benchmark case. We use the benchmark calibration as the low PUD case.Figure 3, (e)–(h), shows that the negative relation between utilization and liquidity is substantially stronger for the high PUD firms in the bad state. This is because the firm’s collateral capacity is more limited in the bad state, and it relies more on the VCU mechanism to manage liquidity. Because the TCC mechanism leads to the positive relation between hedging and expected profitability, it is not surprising to find this relation to be more positive for the high PUD ratio firms, as shown by the bigger difference in hedge ratios between (g) and (h) for the high PUD firms. These observations give rise to the following hypotheses. The negative relation between utilization and liquidity is stronger among firms with higher PUD ratio. The positive relation between hedging and expected profitability is stronger among firms with higher PUD ratio.
article.4. Empirical Design and Data: In this section, we describe the construction of our data set on firms’ capital utilization, production, and hedging. We then discuss our empirical strategy to assess how firm liquidity and expected profitability affect hedging and capital utilization. Our sample comprises publicly traded independent oil and gas producers. This sample is appealing to test our model for several reasons. First, we can quantify the degree to which these firms hedge their output price risk because of detailed contract-level disclosures. Second, because the output price risk is the main business risk for these firms, risk management should a priori be an important consideration for this sample of firms. Third, studying firms in the same industry helps rule out industry-level explanations for our results. Finally, connecting firms’ capital utilization rate and capital depreciation rate in the VCU mechanism is straightforward, despite being a challenging task in many other settings. 4.1. Data We collect hedging data from 10-K filings of public independent oil and gas producers in the United States. The sample period is between 2002 and 2016 due to hedging data availability. Financial reporting standards for derivative instruments are formally established only after Statements of Financial Accounting Standards No. 133 (SFAS 133). Prior to this standard, commodity related derivatives that have physical settlements are not required to be disclosed. After it, firms are required to disclose all derivative positions on the balance sheet and the changes in their fair value in the income statement or shareholders’ equity, including the ones used for hedging activities. All our sample companies adopted the standard by 2002. We exclude integrated oil and gas producers because their refinery segment serves as an operational hedge for the exploratin and production segment and thus greatly complicates the calculation of hedge ratio. In contrast, independent producers rely almost exclusively on derivative contracts for hedging. Therefore, we measure hedge ratio by fraction of production that is hedged by derivative contracts to test our model predictions. FollowingAlmeida et al. (2019), we also take into account the fixed-price physical delivery contracts between the producers and customers. Online Appendix B describes the hedging data collection process in detail. We obtain the spot prices for West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil and Henry Hub natural gas from the U.S. Energy Information Administration. We collect financial data from Compustat. We collect product-level production, proved reserves, and unproved reserves for each firm-year from Bloomberg. Our measure of the capital utilization rate is the production to proved reserves ratio. Because production depletes proved reserves but not unproved reserves (see Online Appendix B.4), our definition measures the fraction of the total extractable commodity that a firm chooses to produce in a given year. We validate our empirical measure by comparing the aggregate utilization rate for our sample firms with a measure of capacity utilization for the oil and gas extraction industry (NAICS 211) collected and published by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System.11This FRED series measures capacity utilization as output scaled by a capacity index where the capacity index “…capture[s] the concept of sustainable maximum output-the greatest level of output a plant can maintain within the framework of a realistic work schedule, after factoring in normal downtime and assuming sufficient availability of inputs to operate the capital in place”FED (2022). In Figure X.3 in the Online Appendix, we plot these two annual time series of utilization. Because the FRED series scales the output by the sustainable maximum output within a year, whereas our measure scales the output by the proved reserves (the total commercially recoverable output from a given date forward), our utilization rate has a lower mean than the FRED series due to the different denominators used. Nevertheless, both measures have a similar volatility and a correlation of 0.43, validating the relevance of our empirical proxy. Table X.3 in the Online Appendix reports the definitions of key empirical variables.Table 2presents summary statistics on our final sample of 851 firm-year observations covering 104 unique firms. The median hedge ratio is 0.452, which is higher than the ratio found in prior studies (Haushalter 2000,Jin and Jorion 2006). This is partly due to the difference in sample periods and partly due to our exclusion of integrated producers whose refinery business serves as an operational hedge and thus a substitute for financial hedges. Only about 10% of firm-years in our sample have a hedge ratio equal to zero, suggesting that the majority of firms in our sample actively manage output price risk. Figure X.4 in the Online Appendix plots the average hedge ratio and the average price, for oil and natural gas separately, in each year of our sample. It shows that oil and gas markets experience both bull and bear periods during our sample period, alleviating the concern that our results are specific to bull or bear periods of the energy market. We also find significant variation in utilization rate for our sample firms. For the median firm-year, production averages 8.7% of reserves, whereas the standard deviation of the utilization rate is 7.1%. Table 2.Summary Statistics Table 2.Summary Statistics Notes.This table presents the summary statistics of the key variables. Variable definitions are in Table X.3 in the Online Appendix. The sample period is from 2002 to 2016. The median book asset value of our sample firms is $1.87 billion. These companies are profitable with a median operating profitability of 13.7%. At the same time, the median cash holding of these companies is only 1.8%, the book leverage is 31.2%, and a dividend is paid in about 55% of all firm-years. These numbers suggest that oil and gas producers have substantial leverage and save relatively little in cash. There are no firm-years with AAA or AA credit ratings and only 17.5% of firm-years have the investment-grade credit rating. 4.2. Empirical Design Our empirical strategy is guided by the testable implications discussed in Section 2.4. The two key state variables in our theory are expected profitability and firm liquidity, which jointly determine firms’ utilization rate and hedge ratio. We first discuss how we measure these two state variables and then explain our identification strategy. We use the commodity spot price as the proxy for the expected profitability. Because our sample firms often produce both oil and natural gas, we computed the weighted spot price at the firm-year level with the weights being the firm’s production of the two commodities and refer to it as the output price. The variation in output price is mainly driven by the time series variation in oil and natural gas prices after controlling for firm fixed effects, because the firms’ production mix is relatively stable over time. Oil and gas prices are determined in the global market and the independent E&P firms in our sample are price-takers.12Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that the commodity price affects our sample firms’ production and hedging decisions rather than the other way around. Nevertheless, in our robustness tests, we address the reverse causality concern that these firms can manipulate the commodity price by changing their production and hedging policies. Firm liquidity in our model is defined as cash, short-term investments, and unrealized hedging gains and losses, net of its borrowing. Our corresponding empirical measure is Compustat cash and short-term investments (che), plus our hand-collected unrealized hedging gains, minus Compustat long-term debt (dltt) and debt in current liabilities (dlc) scaled by total assets. Our liquidity measure is the commonly used net cash measure (Hennessy and Whited 2007), except that we supplement it with the unrealized hedging gains.13We do that for two important reasons. First, our model considers hedging while theirs do not, making the inclusion of the hedging profits necessary. Second, oil and gas firms hedge a substantial portion of their production, and thus empirically hedging gains are an important component of firm liquidity. There is a concern that liquidity and our outcome variables, capital utilization and hedging, can be simultaneously affected by an unobserved firm characteristic. We address this concern by employing unrealized hedging gains scaled by total assets as a novel instrument for liquidity. Scaling by assets accounts for size effects. Unrealized hedging gains are mark-to-market profits on long-term (longer than one year) derivative contracts that are still outstanding at the end of the fiscal year. Because exogenous changes in commodity prices result inunpredictablegains and losses on pre-existing hedging positions established at different points of time during the past fiscal year, the unrealized hedging gains cause exogenous variation in liquidity across firms with different pre-existing hedging positions.14 For unrealized hedging gains to be a plausible instrument for liquidity, the relevance condition and the exclusion restriction must be satisfied. The relevance condition requires our instrument to be correlated with liquidity. This condition is satisfied because the mark-to-market nature of these derivative contracts means that unrealized hedging gains can be readily converted into cash and thus arepartof the firm’s liquidity position.15In our sample, the scaled unrealized hedging gains have a standard deviation of 4.5% (Table 2), indicating that unrealized hedging gains can cause substantial variation in liquidity across firms. The unpredictable nature of price changes is critical for our exclusion restriction assumption that unrealized hedging gains are orthogonal to unobserved firm characteristics that might affect capital utilization and hedging. Despite some in-sample evidence supporting mean reversion and other types of predictability in oil prices,Alquist et al. (2013, p. 454)comprehensively evaluate the out-of-sample accuracy of oil price forecast models in the literature and conclude that “beyond the 3-month horizon… the no-change forecast for all practical purposes remains the most accurate model for forecasting the nominal price of oil in real time.” This means the price level is highly predictable (i.e., prices in the future is in expectation equal to the current price), but the changes in price are not predictable. In practice, successfully trading on the changes in oil price in real time is difficult.16To the best of our knowledge, there is no robust evidence showing that oil and gas producers have superior ability in timing the commodity derivatives markets. The fact that the unrealized hedging gains have a median value about zero in our sample (Table 2) also supports this assumption. Therefore, two concerns can be alleviated. First is that our scaled unrealized hedging gain measure may be correlated with future hedge ratio, other than through liquidity, because hedging is persistent. We regress the scaled unrealized hedging gains on lagged hedge ratio and find no evidence of a significant relationship. This is not surprising because scaled unrealized hedging gains depend onprice changes,which should not be predictable by lagged variables. Second is that our results are driven by a mechanical negative correlation between our scaled unrealized hedging gains and the level of price. Empirically, there is no statistically significant negative relation between unrealized hedging gains and the lagged oil and gas price in the data. We further address this concern by including the level of price, a state variable in our model, in the regression. Finally, although our identification strategy alleviates the concern related to omitted firm choices and reverse causality, we cannot rule out the possibility that oil and natural gas prices and unrealized hedging gains are proxies for external economic forces outside of our model that also drive firms’ risk management behaviors (e.g., macroeconomic risks). Such concerns can only be addressed by modeling these economic forces and examining the resulting description of the empirical patterns, which we leave to future research.

Average Effect of Liquidity on Capital Utilization Hypothesis U1states that the next-period capital utilization rate is negatively correlated with current liquidity. To test this hypothesis, we start by estimating the following linear model with ordinary least squares (OLS): The dependent variable,UtilizationRatei,t+1, is the ratio of the total production in yeart +1 scaled by the total proved reserves at the end of yeart.Liquidityitis net cash plus unrealized hedging gains scaled by total assets. We control for firm size throughout our analysis by including the log of the book value of lagged assets as a covariate. This is because most hedging occurs through over-the-counter markets, and economies of scale in hedging leads to a significant size effect on hedging intensity (Doshi et al. 2018). We also control for lagged leverage. We incorporate year fixed effects,λt, to control for all industry-wide time-varying common shocks, including the effect of expected profitability. The regression includes firm-CEO fixed effects,γi, to account for time-invariant firm characteristics and managerial skill and preferences. The standard errors are clustered at both the year and firm levels. The coefficient on unrealized hedging gains is negative and significant at the 5% level, which is again consistent withHypothesis U1. Heterogeneous Effects on Hedging Next, we testHypothesis H3that the positive relationship between hedging and liquidity is stronger when aggregate volatility is higher. As with our test ofHypothesis U1, we split our sample into years with below and above median values of realized oil price volatility. We estimate the model inEquation (14)separately on these two subsamples and present the 2SLS results in the first two columns ofTable 6. Alternative Explanations We conduct several robustness tests to examine alternative explanations. First, we provide additional corroborative evidence regarding the intertemporal trade off channel of the VCU mechanism. Specifically, we followJordà (2005)and show that the impulse response of the level and volatility of sales to changes in utilization is positive in the first year but negative in the longer term. Second, we confirm the importance of variability in utilization by showing that the hedging-liquidity (hedging-profitability) relationship is weaker (stronger) when utilization is more flexible. Third, our results are robust to using net worth instead of liquidity. Using profitability measures like gross margin or operating margin instead of spot prices also leads to similar conclusions. Fourth, risk-shifting can lead to a positive correlation between liquidity and hedging if the most distressed firms hedge less (Jensen and Meckling 1976,Leland 1998). We show our results remain quantitatively similar even after excluding the most distressed 10% of firm-years in our sample. Fifth, albeit unlikely, there is the concern our sample firms can influence oil prices. The results are robust to using theKilian (2009)index as an instrument for oil price. Sixth, mean reversion in prices can generate a positive relation between hedging and current prices, but it cannot explain the more positive relation for firms with more time-varying collateral capacity. Seventh, since we control for firm-CEO fixed effects throughout, our results cannot be explained by differences in market timing abilities across firms or by differences in managers’ preferences regarding hedging. Finally, serial correlation in hedging behavior is unlikely to drive the results because we find similar results when we include lagged hedge ratio as a control variable. All these tests are detailed in Online Appendix C.
article.6. Conclusion: We extend the unified risk management model inBolton et al. (2011)by analyzing how endogenous capital utilization decisions interact with firms’ investment, financing, and hedging decisions in the presence of time-varying collateral capacity. Our model shows that both variable capital utilization (VCU) and time-varying collateral capacity (TCC) are important for firms’ optimal risk management policy. Using a comprehensive data set of capital utilization and hedging for independent oil and gas producers, we test the novel predictions of our model. Using the output price as a proxy for expected profitability and the unrealized gains on pre-existing hedging positions to identify exogenous shocks to firm liquidity, we find that capital utilization is negatively correlated with liquidity, whereas hedging is positively correlated with liquidity and expected profitability. The negative utilization-liquidity relation is the key implication of the VCU mechanism. This relation is stronger among firms with tighter collateral constraints and weaker during periods of high volatility. The positive hedging-expected profitability relation is the key implication of the TCC mechanism. This relation is stronger among firms whose collateral constraints covary more strongly with expected profitability. These empirical patterns are consistent with our model but are challenging to explain with the existing theories. Our results highlight that firms’ risk management policies depend critically on the firms’ production decision and the state-dependent nature of the collateral constraint.
